article_title: I can run my entire home lab on Proxmox LXCs while using a fraction of the resources VMs would need
core_thesis: Proxmox Linux Containers (LXCs) offer a lightweight alternative to virtual machines (VMs) for home lab environments, especially when running Linux-based services. By sharing the host kernel, LXCs drastically reduce CPU, memory, and storage overhead compared to full VMs, while still providing isolation and supporting advanced features like GPU passthrough and snapshot backups. The author argues that for most self-hosting and experimentation needs, LXCs are preferable to VMs due to their efficiency and ease of deployment, though VMs remain necessary for specialized operating systems or stronger isolation.
detailed_summary: The article explains how Proxmox LXCs can run an entire home lab using far fewer resources than traditional VMs. LXCs share the host's kernel, so they don't need to virtualize a full OS, resulting in minimal CPU and memory overhead. The author demonstrates that a weak 2014 laptop with a 2-core i5 and 4GB RAM can run over 10 LXCs, whereas it would struggle with even a single GUI VM. Storage is also minimal—templates take only a few hundred MBs—and startup times are rapid. Proxmox provides many pre-built community templates via the Helper-Scripts repo, covering everything from media servers to automation tools. The author also highlights advanced use cases: an Ollama LXC for local LLMs with GPU passthrough (sharing a Pascal GPU across multiple LXCs without SR-IOV), and a Tailscale subnet router LXC for remote access. However, LXCs have limitations: they cannot be live-migrated in a cluster, offer less isolation than VMs, and running Docker inside LXCs has caveats. The article concludes that unless you need specialized OSes, stronger security, or features like Home Assistant VM, LXCs are the better choice for most home lab workloads.

entities.name: Ollama

entities.name: Lenovo G510
entities.role: Example low-end laptop used to demonstrate LXC resource efficiency

key_data_points: LXCs share the host kernel, eliminating the need for a separate OS kernel per container, which drastically reduces resource consumption compared to VMs. (🟢 Relevant to homelab: allows running many services on low-power hardware.)

A 2014 Lenovo G510 with 2-core i5-4200M and 4GB RAM can run over 10 LXCs, while it could only handle a single CLI-based DietPi VM. (🟢 Demonstrates extreme efficiency; useful for repurposing old hardware.)
LXC templates consume only a few hundred MBs of storage, versus dozens of GBs for a VM disk image. (🟢 Saves storage space in homelab.)
Proxmox supports PCI passthrough for LXCs, allowing a single GPU to be shared across multiple containers without SR-IOV. (🟢 Enables GPU-accelerated workloads like LLMs in lightweight containers.)
LXCs cannot be live-migrated in a Proxmox cluster, which is a limitation for high-availability setups. (🟢 Important for planning critical services.)
Docker can run inside LXCs but with caveats; performance is reliable but not as seamless as on bare metal or VMs. (🟢 Relevant for users who want Docker in containers.)
methodology_steps: 1. Install Proxmox on your server hardware (e.g., old laptop, mini-PC, or SBC).
2. Access the Proxmox web interface and navigate to the 'Create CT' button to start creating an LXC.
3. Choose a template: either use the Proxmox VE Helper-Scripts repo for pre-configured templates (e.g., media server, automation) or select a TurnKey Linux template for a basic distro.
4. For a DIY approach, select a Debian (or other distro) template from the built-in repository. Configure CPU cores, memory, and storage (minimal values work).
5. After creation, start the LXC and install your desired services (e.g., Ollama, Tailscale) via SSH or console.
6. For GPU passthrough, enable PCI passthrough in Proxmox for the LXC and attach the GPU device. Multiple LXCs can share the same GPU without SR-IOV.
7. For remote access, set up a Tailscale subnet router LXC with elevated network privileges to route traffic to all containers and VMs.
8. Use Proxmox Backup Server (PBS) to take snapshots of LXCs just like VMs for easy recovery.
conclusions: LXCs are significantly more resource-efficient than VMs for Linux-based workloads, making them ideal for home labs with limited hardware. The author's experience shows that even a decade-old laptop can run a dozen containers, whereas VMs would be impractical.
Advanced features like GPU passthrough and snapshot backups work well with LXCs, enabling use cases like local LLM hosting and remote access without the overhead of VMs.

However, LXCs are not a complete replacement for VMs: they offer weaker isolation, cannot be live-migrated, and may have compatibility issues with Docker. Users should evaluate their need for security, high availability, and specific OS requirements before choosing LXCs over VMs.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your homelab and DevOps interests, as it provides a practical guide to maximizing resource efficiency using Proxmox LXCs. You can apply these techniques to run multiple services on low-power hardware, freeing up resources for other experiments. The GPU passthrough and Tailscale subnet router examples offer concrete ideas for enhancing your lab's capabilities. As a programmer, you can leverage LXCs to quickly spin up isolated environments for testing code or deploying self-hosted tools without the overhead of full VMs.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a dedicated LXC for Docker to run containerized applications, as mentioned in the comments. This combines the efficiency of LXCs with the ecosystem of Docker images. Also, explore the Proxmox VE Helper-Scripts repo for one-command deployments of popular services like Jellyfin, Nextcloud, or Pi-hole—this can save hours of manual configuration.
